key_insight: A variational nonlocal phase-field formulation models dynamic fracture by replacing local kinematics and crack regularization with kernel-based nonlocal mechanics, so crack evolution emerges without explicit crack tracking or a prescribed internal length.
engineering_relevance: The method provides a more flexible fracture simulation framework for elastic solids, capturing initiation, branching, shear fracture, and fragmentation in dynamic loading scenarios with finite-element implementation.
method: Nonlocal kinematics with kernel-dependent function spaces reduce regularity requirements and recover local theory as interaction radius shrinks.
A nonlocal crack-surface functional acts as an integral analogue of Ambrosio–Tortorelli regularization, with crack width governed by the nonlocal domain.
The coupled system uses a nonlocal momentum balance plus irreversible phase-field gradient flow, solved by scalar auxiliary-variable and staggered time integration schemes.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports successful numerical benchmarks for Mode-I fracture, dynamic branching, Kalthoff–Winkler-type shear fracture, and fragmentation, including crack-tip speeds below 0.6c_R and an inclined shear crack path of about 48°.
watch_item: Accuracy and physical length-scale calibration depend on the chosen nonlocal interaction kernel/domain, so implementation sensitivity and parameter selection may affect predictive robustness.
paper_title: A Variational Nonlocal Phase-Field Model for Dynamic Fracture in Elastic Solids
